content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most people think AI is changing the way we work.
I think it’s doing something far more interesting.
It’s revealing how differently people think
When ChatGPT first became popular, people started asking the same question.
“What’s the best AI tool?”
Then it became:
“What’s the best prompt?”
Today it’s:
“Which model should I subscribe to?”
The conversation keeps changing, but it’s almost always about the tool.
I think we’re asking the wrong question.
After watching hundreds of people use AI in completely different ways, I’ve noticed something strange.
Two people can open the exact same AI model, type in similar requests, and walk away with completely different results.
One finishes a proposal in twenty minutes.
The other spends two hours rewriting mediocre output before deciding AI is overrated.
Same AI.
Same task.
Completely different outcome.
The difference wasn’t the AI.
It was the quality of thinking behind the questions.
Maybe the biggest AI divide isn’t between people using different models.
Maybe it’s between people asking better questions.
The Real Bottleneck Isn’t AI
Let’s get one thing out of the way.
AI has real limitations. It hallucinates. It states wrong information with total confidence. It misunderstands context, misses nuance, sometimes invents sources that sound entirely plausible.
Those are real problems.
But for most knowledge workers, they aren’t the actual reason they’re getting mediocre results.
AI doesn’t distort your thinking. It amplifies it.
A vague request gets you a vague answer. A flawed assumption gets built on happily, without objection, dressed up in confident prose. If you don’t know what a good outcome looks like, the AI doesn’t either. It isn’t withholding that from you. Nobody gave it that in the first place.
The tool isn’t creating the confusion. It’s exposing it.
There’s a phrase programmers learn to fear more than any error message: undefined behavior. Code that’s technically broken but doesn’t crash. It just runs, quietly, and produces something plausible-looking that turns out to be wrong in a way nobody catches until much later.
That’s what a bad prompt gets back. AI rarely throws an error for an unclear question. It just returns undefined behavior dressed up as an answer: confident, complete-looking, and wrong in a way that won’t surface until after you’ve already used it.
Think of AI less like a genie and more like a mirror. A blurry question doesn’t produce a clear reflection, no matter how good the mirror is.
We’ve Seen This Divide Before
This gap isn’t new. It has almost nothing to do with AI.
Picture two people handed the exact same notebook on the same day. One fills it with answers. Facts collected, lessons learned, boxes ticked off. The other fills it with questions. What am I missing here. Why does this actually work. What would prove me wrong.
A year later, set the two notebooks side by side. The first one looks impressive. Page after page of tidy conclusions. The second one looks unfinished, almost restless.
Ask which person understands more, though, and it isn’t close. The one who spent the year writing questions has been quietly training the one skill that actually compounds: noticing what you don’t know yet.
AI runs on the same notebook. It pays out on the quality of the question long before it ever pays out on the quality of the answer. Hand it a sharp question and it hands back something worth using. Hand it a flat one and it hands back something that only looks finished.
For most of history, that difference stayed private. You could fill a notebook with tidy answers for an entire career and no one would ever hand you a stopwatch and prove which habit actually served you.
AI hands you the stopwatch.
It doesn’t make anyone a shallower thinker. It just removes the years of organizational cover that used to sit between a bad habit of mind and its consequences. In most jobs, sloppy thinking gets diluted: by committees, by time, by a manager quietly cleaning things up before anyone notices. AI skips all of that. It just answers.
That’s the real shift nobody’s naming out loud. AI isn’t replacing intelligence. It’s exposing it. Two equally hardworking, equally sincere people can sit down at the same tool and walk away with completely different results, because the machine has no interest in flattering anyone’s self-image. It just answers the question you actually asked. Not the one you meant to ask.
The Prompt Isn’t the Problem
Imagine asking AI:
“Write me a marketing strategy.”
Technically, that’s a prompt. Practically, it’s a request with almost no useful constraints. It’s the AI equivalent of asking a contractor to “build me a house” with no lot, no budget, and no blueprint.
Now compare it with this:

Get Noor Bakht’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.
Remember me for faster sign in

“I’m launching a skincare brand aimed at women aged 25–35. My budget is $2,000. My goal is customer acquisition rather than brand awareness. Create a three-month marketing strategy with measurable KPIs. Before answering, tell me what information you’re missing.”
Both people are using the same technology.
But only one has done the difficult work before typing.
That’s not prompt engineering. That’s problem definition. It happened before either of them ever opened the chat window.
By the time your cursor is blinking in the box, the real work is already finished or it isn’t. The typing is just the part you can see.
The best prompts aren’t usually longer. They’re clearer. Which is another way of saying the person writing them had already done the thinking somewhere else first.
Here’s a real example from my work. I once asked an AI model why a batch of products was failing inspection more often than usual, and it gave me two confident, well-organized paragraphs of general advice about tightening quality control. It wasn’t exactly wrong. It was answering a problem I hadn’t actually described. So I stopped, wrote down what the defect actually was, where in the process it was appearing, and what had changed recently, then asked again with all three included. The second answer was a single paragraph. It pointed to the likely cause in the very first sentence. Nothing about the model had changed in those ten minutes. I had.
We’ve Become Obsessed With Better Prompts
Prompt libraries. Prompt marketplaces. Prompt engineering courses. Prompt hacks.
They’re useful. But they’re solving a downstream problem.
The upstream problem is simpler than that, and less flattering: most people never defined what they were trying to accomplish in the first place.
A better prompt can’t fix unclear thinking. It can only phrase the confusion more politely.
That’s why copying someone else’s prompt so rarely produces someone else’s results. You inherited their sentence. You didn’t inherit the thinking that made the sentence necessary.
This is what makes AI different from every productivity trend before it. The technology itself is now almost free to copy. Anyone can open the same model, pay for the same subscription, paste in the same prompt someone else posted online.
Judgment doesn’t democratize the same way. It can’t be copy-pasted, subscribed to, or unlocked with a better plan. The gap between people isn’t closing as AI spreads. It’s showing up more clearly than it ever did before.
The Skill AI Can’t Replace
This isn’t just true for AI.
The best consultants don’t jump straight to solutions. They spend more time defining the problem.
The best researchers challenge their own assumptions before looking for evidence.
The best operators map the process before trying to improve it.
The pattern repeats because it was never really about consultants, researchers, or operators. It’s about where people choose to spend their attention: before the visible work starts, or after.
That makes me think the most valuable skill in the AI era isn’t prompting. It’s judgment.
Knowing when an answer is incomplete. Knowing what context is missing. Knowing which assumptions deserve to be challenged. Knowing when to ask another question instead of accepting the first answer.
Those aren’t AI skills. They’re thinking skills.
The people who benefit most from AI won’t necessarily be the ones who know every new feature. They’ll be the ones who know how to think before they ask.
A Simple Test Before You Ask AI Anything
Try this the next time you’re about to type a request into AI. Don’t touch the keyboard yet. Answer these five questions out loud, or on paper, first:

• What problem am I actually trying to solve?

• What assumptions am I already making?

• What information is AI missing?

• What would a useful answer actually look like?

• How will I verify that the answer is correct?
Those five questions will improve your output more reliably than any clever prompt template. The quality of an answer is a ceiling set by the quality of the question underneath it. No amount of polishing the surface raises that ceiling.
The Real Competitive Advantage
AI will keep getting better. Models will become faster, more accurate, more affordable.
Eventually, everyone will have access to similar tools. That means access stops being an advantage.
Judgment doesn’t expire the same way. The ability to define a problem clearly, question your own assumptions, weigh evidence honestly, and notice when an answer sounds convincing but isn’t. None of that gets commoditized just because the tool underneath it gets cheaper.
AI isn’t replacing that skill. It’s pricing it, in public, for the first time.
Maybe that’s the real reason some people call AI life-changing while others call it disappointing. They’re using the exact same technology. They’re just bringing different habits of thinking to it. AI didn’t invent that difference. It just stopped letting anyone look away from it.
So perhaps the most useful question isn’t “which AI model should I use.”
It’s “am I asking questions that deserve great answers?”
Here’s the part most people don’t want to hear: AI didn’t lower the bar for good thinking. It removed the hiding places for bad thinking. There’s no slow coworker to blame, no vague market to shrug at, no long feedback delay to blur the line between your effort and your result. It’s just you, a blinking cursor, and an answer that’s only ever as good as the question underneath it.
Everyone’s going to have the same AI eventually. Not everyone is going to have done the thinking first.
That’s not a tooling gap. That’s the whole gap. The mirror was never the problem. It was just the first one clear enough to make you look.
I’m curious where you disagree. If you think AI is changing something other than the way we think, I’d love to hear your perspective.
